summary: Added a planning-only translation quality workflow that defines placeholder status, reviewer expectations, locale-specific review risks, acceptance criteria, and future translation replacement slice rules without changing localization resources or product code.

contract_status_changes.before: Localization docs stated that non-English locales were English placeholders, but did not define reviewer status labels, acceptance criteria, or how future slices may claim translation quality.
contract_status_changes.after: docs/workflows/localization-translation-quality.md defines translation status labels, native/fluent review expectations, acceptance criteria, locale-specific risks, terminology to review deliberately, and prohibited translation-slice behavior.

contract_status_changes.before: The repo described non-English values as placeholders but did not state the exact current placeholder baseline.
contract_status_changes.after: The plan documents that all 18 non-English locales currently match English for 282 static keys and share the same 11 plural resources, so future translation slices can distinguish placeholder replacement from key drift.

contract_status_changes.before: A future agent could treat parity, running-app smoke, or screenshot proof as translation quality.
contract_status_changes.after: The plan explicitly states that machine/AI translation is draft-only, reviewer acceptance is required for quality claims, and parity/smoke/screenshot proof do not prove translation quality alone.

residual_risks: No non-English translations were replaced in this slice.
No native or fluent reviewer has accepted any non-English locale values.
The plan is doc-only and does not add mechanical detection for placeholder percentage or reviewer status.
Device, TestFlight, and native-speaker review proof remain future work.
recommended_next_reason: Before replacing locale files, generate a small reviewer-ready export of English keys, current placeholders, product terminology, and status labels so a human/native review can happen outside the app source without drifting from the repo contract.
